DNS

DNS (Domain Name System) is a distributed, hierarchical naming system that translates human-readable domain names (for example, google.com) into IP addresses. Without DNS, users would have to remember IP addresses instead of domain names.

How It Works

  • The client sends a DNS query to a Recursive Resolver.
  • If the answer is not cached, the resolver queries the DNS hierarchy.
  • Root Server identifies the Top-Level Domain (TLD), such as .com or .org.
  • TLD Server identifies the domain's Authoritative Name Server.
  • Authoritative Name Server returns the requested DNS record, such as A, AAAA, MX, TXT, or NS.
  • The resolver caches the response according to the TTL (Time To Live).
  • The resolver returns the IP address to the client.
  • The client uses the IP address to establish a connection with the destination server.

Ports

UDP/53 - Default protocol for most DNS queries. It is fast and connectionless.

TCP/53 - Used for Zone Transfers (AXFR/IXFR), DNS responses that exceed the UDP size limit, or retrying after truncation.

Common DNS Record Types

  • A - Domain to IPv4 address.
  • AAAA - Domain to IPv6 address.
  • CNAME - Alias to another hostname.
  • MX - Mail server.
  • NS - Authoritative name servers.
  • TXT - Text records such as SPF, DKIM, verification tokens, and other metadata.
  • PTR - Reverse DNS, IP to hostname.
  • SOA - Zone configuration and authority information.
  • SRV - Service location, such as LDAP or Kerberos.

Important Notes

  • DNS responses are cached according to their TTL (Time To Live).
  • A Recursive Resolver performs lookups on behalf of clients.
  • An Authoritative Name Server stores the official DNS records for a domain.
  • Zone Transfer (AXFR) can expose an organization's entire DNS zone if misconfigured.
  • Common attack techniques include DNS Spoofing, Cache Poisoning, DNS Tunneling, Zone Transfers, and Subdomain Enumeration.
  • DNS is one of the most valuable information sources during Reconnaissance.

Query Whois Records On The Domain

whois megacorpone.com

Query DNS Records

host -t {a|txt|ns|mx} megacorpone.com
dig {a|txt|ns|mx} domain.com

Query DNS Records From A Specific DNS Server

dig {a|txt|ns|mx} domain.com @ns1.domain.com

DNS Zone Transfer (AXFR)

host -l megacorpone.com ns1.megacorpone.com
dnsrecon -d megacorpone.com -t axfr @ns2.megacorpone.com

Automated Tool

dnsenum offseclab.io --threads 100

Resources

MITRE ATT&CK T1071.004
